title: Can you save money charging a home battery on a cheap night rate?
description: Charging a home battery at 7p overnight and using it at 26p can save hundreds a year. The real maths, payback times and the catches that shrink it.
blurb: What night-rate battery arbitrage genuinely saves, with honest payback maths and the catches most sales pitches skip.
lede: Buy electricity at 7p a kilowatt hour while you sleep, store it in a battery, then use it during the day instead of paying 26p. That gap of roughly 19p per unit is the whole idea behind battery arbitrage, and on paper it looks like free money. In practice the savings are real but smaller than the headline gap suggests, because round-trip losses, your actual daytime usage and the cost of the battery itself all take a slice. This guide works through the genuine numbers so you can decide whether a battery without solar, or alongside it, earns its keep in your house.
short_answer: Yes, genuinely. Charging a typical 10 kWh battery at an EV-style overnight rate of 7p and using it against a 26.11p day rate saves roughly 18p per usable unit, which works out at £400 to £650 a year for most households. At an installed cost of £4,000 to £7,000 the battery typically pays for itself in 7 to 10 years on a cheap overnight tariff, but only in 12 years or more on a standard Economy 7 rate, so the tariff you can actually get matters as much as the battery.

sections.h2: How charging a battery overnight actually saves money
sections.html:
The principle is simple. Time-of-use tariffs sell electricity cheaply at night, when national demand is low, and charge more during the day. A home battery lets you buy when it is cheap and spend when it is dear, without changing anything about how you live. The battery charges between roughly midnight and 5am, then your kettle, oven, washing machine and lights draw from it through the day instead of from the grid.

Your saving per unit is the gap between the two rates, minus losses. Two numbers decide everything:

  • The spread. On the July to September 2026 price cap, the standard unit rate is 26.11p per kWh. EV-style overnight rates run at about 7 to 9p, and the cheapest smart tariffs dip to around 5.5p. That is a spread of 17 to 21p per unit.
  • Round-trip efficiency. Lithium batteries and their inverters lose energy going in and coming out. A realistic figure for a modern home system is about 90%, so every 10 kWh you buy at night delivers roughly 9 kWh back into the house.

Put those together and electricity bought at 7p costs about 7.8p per kWh by the time you use it, against 26.11p from the grid at midday. That 18.3p gap, multiplied by every unit you shift, is your saving. The same logic powers Economy 7, the original night tariff, though its spread is much narrower, as the table below shows.

sections.h2: The numbers: what each night rate saves you
sections.html:
The table assumes a battery with 90% round-trip efficiency and a daytime rate of 26.11p per kWh. The two right-hand columns show the annual saving if you shift 6 kWh a day (a modest household) or 9 kWh a day (a fully cycled 10 kWh battery in a higher-use home).

Overnight rateEffective cost per kWh usedSaving per kWhPer year, 6 kWh/dayPer year, 9 kWh/day
5.5p (cheapest smart EV tariffs)6.1p20.0p£438£657
7p (typical EV overnight rate)7.8p18.3p£401£601
9p (dearer EV overnight rate)10.0p16.1p£353£529
13p (typical Economy 7 night rate)14.4p11.7p£256£384

Against a daytime rate of 26.11p per kWh, the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026. Overnight rates are typical market examples; check your own tariff.

Two honest caveats sit behind these figures. First, you can only save on electricity you actually use during expensive hours. A home that uses 8 kWh a day in total, some of it already overnight, cannot shift 9 kWh no matter how big the battery is. Size the saving to your real daytime consumption, which your smart meter's in-home display or supplier app will show you.

Second, Economy 7 deserves a closer look before you build a plan on it. Its day rate usually sits above the standard cap rate, so a battery too small to cover all your daytime use can leave the uncovered units costing more than they did before. The EV-style smart tariffs avoid this trap because their daytime rates are normally at or near the cap.

sections.h2: What a battery costs and how long payback takes
sections.html:
A retrofit home battery with around 9 to 13 kWh of usable capacity typically costs £4,000 to £7,000 installed in 2026, depending on brand, size and whether your consumer unit needs work. Battery storage currently qualifies for 0% VAT when professionally installed, which is already reflected in most quotes, but the relief is time-limited so check the current position on the gov.uk energy-saving materials page before you sign.

Set those costs against the table above and the payback picture looks like this:

  • Cheap overnight tariff, well-matched battery: £500 to £650 a year saved on a £4,500 to £5,500 system gives payback in roughly 7 to 10 years.
  • Modest daytime use: at £400 a year, a £5,000 system takes about 12 years.
  • Economy 7 only: at £256 to £384 a year, payback stretches to 13 years or beyond, which is longer than most battery warranties.

Most lithium batteries carry a 10 to 12 year warranty, often expressed as around 6,000 charge cycles. Daily cycling for arbitrage uses about 365 cycles a year, so the warranty comfortably covers a decade of this. The battery does not die at the warranty's end, but its capacity will have faded, typically to around 70 or 80% of new. A payback period that finishes inside the warranty is the honest test, and on a true cheap-rate tariff it generally does. We work through the full sums, including pairing with solar, in our guide to battery storage payback.

sections.h2: The catches that shrink the savings
sections.html:
None of these kill the idea, but every one of them trims the headline number, and a sales quote that ignores all of them is not being straight with you.

  • Tariff eligibility. The cheapest overnight rates, the 5.5p to 7p ones, are EV tariffs, and most suppliers require you to own an electric car or a compatible smart charger to sign up. A few suppliers now offer smart tariffs aimed at battery owners without a car, but the choice is thinner and the rates are usually a little higher. Check eligibility before you buy the battery, not after. Our guide to EV electricity tariffs explains how the main types work.
  • Usable capacity is less than the headline. A battery sold as 13.5 kWh might offer 12 kWh usable, and the inverter may cap how fast it can discharge. A 3 kW inverter cannot run a 7 kW shower or a 9 kW hob alone, so heavy loads still pull some grid electricity at the day rate.
  • Round-trip losses are real. The 10% loss in our maths is typical, but a cheap system or a battery in a cold garage can lose more.
  • Winter and summer differ. Arbitrage itself works year-round, which is a genuine advantage over solar-only savings. But if your daytime electricity use is low in summer, the battery has less expensive consumption to displace.
  • Standing charges do not move. You still pay 57.19p a day for electricity regardless. Arbitrage only attacks the unit-rate part of the bill.
  • Price cap rates change every three months. The spread between night and day rates has stayed wide for years, but it is not guaranteed. A narrower spread lengthens payback.

sections.h2: Who this works for, and who should wait
sections.html:
The strongest case is an EV owner who already has, or can get, a 5.5p to 9p overnight tariff for the car. The cheap rate is already on the meter, so the battery piggybacks on it at no extra tariff cost, and savings of £500 or more a year are realistic. If that is you, the same overnight window also makes charging the car itself dramatically cheaper than daytime rates.

A good case is a household with solar panels, or planning them. The battery then earns twice: it stores surplus solar in summer and does night-rate arbitrage in winter when the panels produce little. That combination is usually the fastest route to payback, and it is covered in detail in our guides to solar battery storage and whether solar is worth it. If you are starting from zero, read solar panels: the basics first, because panels plus battery is often a better use of the same budget than a battery alone.

A marginal case is a home with no EV, no solar and only Economy 7 available. The 11 to 12p spread can still save £250 to £380 a year, but payback runs past the warranty and the higher E7 day rate adds risk. We weigh this scenario fully in is a home battery without solar worth it.

Worth a mention: some suppliers also pay batteries to export at peak times or to provide grid services, and dynamic tariffs that change price every half hour open further room for clever charging. These can add £100 or more a year on top of plain arbitrage, but they need a compatible system and more attention, so treat them as a bonus rather than the core of the business case. Our guide to agile and tracker tariffs explains how the dynamic kind work.

Battery arbitrage on a cheap night rate is one of the few energy investments where the saving is mechanical rather than hopeful. You are not relying on the weather, on changing your habits or on a grant scheme surviving. Buy at 7p, use at 26p, pocket most of the difference, every single day.

The deciding factor is the tariff, not the battery. With access to a genuine 5.5p to 9p overnight rate and a decent amount of daytime consumption, savings of £400 to £650 a year and payback inside the warranty are realistic. Stuck on Economy 7 at 13p, the sums still work but slowly, and you should price up solar panels alongside the battery before committing, because the combined system usually beats either alone. Whatever you do, get your last twelve months of half-hourly usage from your smart meter data first. Ten minutes with real numbers beats any installer's brochure estimate.

faq.q: Do I need solar panels for a home battery to pay off?
faq.a: No. A battery charged purely from a cheap overnight tariff can save £400 to £650 a year on its own if you can access a 5.5p to 9p night rate and use most of the stored energy during the day. That said, solar plus battery usually reaches payback faster than a battery alone, because the battery earns from free solar in summer and cheap night units in winter.

faq.q: Can I get a cheap EV overnight tariff without owning an electric car?
faq.a: Usually not. Most suppliers require an EV or a compatible smart charger on the account for their cheapest overnight rates. A small number of smart tariffs accept home batteries as the qualifying device, and standard Economy 7 is open to anyone, though its night rate of around 13p gives a much smaller saving per unit. Check eligibility with the supplier before buying the battery.

faq.q: How much does charging a 10 kWh battery overnight actually cost?
faq.a: At a 7p overnight rate, filling a 10 kWh battery costs about 70p. After typical round-trip losses it delivers roughly 9 kWh back, which would cost £2.35 from the grid at the 26.11p cap rate. That is a saving of about £1.65 per full cycle, or around £600 a year if you cycle it daily and use all the stored energy.

faq.q: Will daily charging wear the battery out before it pays for itself?
faq.a: Generally no. Daily arbitrage uses about 365 cycles a year, and modern lithium batteries are warranted for around 6,000 cycles or 10 to 12 years. On a genuine cheap overnight rate, payback of 7 to 10 years lands inside the warranty period. Capacity does fade gradually, typically to 70 to 80% of new by the end of the warranty, so build a little headroom into your sizing.

faq.q: Is it better to export from the battery at peak times instead of using the energy myself?
faq.a: Usually self-use wins, because avoiding a 26.11p import beats most export payments. The exception is a tariff with a high peak export rate or paid grid-flexibility events, where exporting for an hour or two in the early evening can earn more than the electricity is worth to you. Treat export income as a bonus on top of the core saving, not the foundation of the purchase.
